data.title: OpenClaw's shrimp farming craze is expanding: Offline creator workshops are about to open.
data.description:
In recent weeks, OpenClaw, once confined to the geek community, has rapidly expanded beyond its core audience. Within the developer community, it's affectionately known as "Lobster," and "lobster farming"—training and debugging AI agents to perform complex tasks—has become one of the hottest practices in the AI ​​era.

This trend is extending from the developer community to financial research and policy perspectives. Financial institutions have adopted it as a powerful tool for investment research, while universities and regulatory agencies are frequently warning of risks related to access control and data security. The explosion of OpenClaw is a microcosm of AI agents moving from "auxiliary tools" to "core productivity tools," and it also makes a core question increasingly clear: How will AI reshape the way we work in the future?

From Tool to Productivity

AI is completing a crucial leap: from "responding to instructions" to "actively executing."

AI agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, can not only understand instructions but also autonomously allocate resources and connect processes to achieve task closure. They enable individuals to build automated workflows, fostering entirely new production methods. However, amidst this hype, key challenges for implementation are becoming apparent: How to train truly usable agents? How to design secure and stable workflows? How to balance efficiency and risk? These questions cannot be fully answered through online discussions; offline experience sharing and practical exchanges become crucial.

An offline creator workshop for "shrimp players"

Against this backdrop, the "OpenClaw Creator Workshop • Skills Bazaar," jointly launched by Jinse Finance × Twinkle × Shuan Academy, will officially open on March 17th from 13:30 to 17:30.

This is not a traditional technical lecture, but a "skills market" focusing on practical application.

The event will focus on four core topics, directly addressing the pain points of AI Agent implementation: How can AI Agents be integrated into real-world workflows to double efficiency? OpenClaw's practical experience and techniques in development and automation. How can AI tools empower creators to overcome productivity bottlenecks? How to build a strong security defense and mitigate data and permission risks when using AI Agents? For experienced AI users, this is an offline platform to exchange advanced techniques and resolve difficulties; for beginners, it's a shortcut to quickly get started and understand AI Agent application scenarios. In the AI ​​era, everyone needs their own "lobster." This workshop is reportedly just the first stop in a series of events. The organizers plan to continue the series in eight core cities—Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Hangzhou, Chengdu, Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai—over the next few months, bringing the best practices of the global AI Agent community to more developers. Technological development has never stopped at tools; it relies even more on sharing and co-creation. The arrival of the AI ​​Agent era is no exception. OpenClaw's "lobster-raising craze" is essentially a preview of future work methods. AI is no longer just a chat window, but a digital assistant capable of executing tasks and managing processes. The process of "raising lobsters" is precisely the process of learning to coexist with AI and mastering future productivity. The AI ​​Agent era has arrived. The OpenClaw Creator Workshop on March 17th marks a crucial juncture in this transformation. It may simply be a technical exchange, but for everyone eager to embrace AI opportunities, it could be a key step in understanding AI and taking control of the future. This offline gathering for "Lobster Players" awaits your participation.

data.title: Bank card payments vs. stablecoin payments: Which is better for smart agents?
data.description:
Author: Jack Simison, NascentTranslated by: Yangz, Techub News

Last Friday, I had a barbecue with one of the maintainers of OpenClaw to discuss which payment channels smart agents should use. The debate lasted only about 30 seconds. He said:

"Anything will do as long as it's not cryptocurrency."

This isn't without reason.

After being plagued by discussions surrounding Moltbook and a series of token issuances, OpenClaw's Discord server has completely banned mentions of cryptocurrency. Every serious discussion about intelligent agent architecture ultimately ends up being accompanied by a token's price code. Therefore, banning the topic is a reasonable response. This association gives serious non-cryptocurrency developers ample reason to view "cryptocurrency" as a burden, not infrastructure. If you're launching an AI product in 2026, the last thing you want is for your payment layer to be burdened by speculation. But this reaction masks a crucial difference. Stablecoins are not speculative tokens. They have no issuance ceremony. They don't go to zero. They are a unit of value that can be settled without banks, transferred without wire transfers, and costs less than a penny per transaction. Peter Steinberger's decision to integrate OpenClaw with CreditClaw (a virtual card solution powered by Stripe) immediately drew criticism from the cryptocurrency community. In 2026, many considered relying on traditional card payment infrastructure obsolete, accusing him of a flawed approach. However, for its specific use case, he was correct. Steinberger's choice to use bank cards was correct. When intelligent agents act as human proxies to do things humans want to do but don't want to do themselves, bank card payment channels are indeed the right choice. The design intent of bank card networks is very clear: to make it convenient for people to use their funds. Users open accounts with issuing banks, which provide cards and credit limits (for credit cards) or linked debit balances (for debit cards). The bank card network then routes transaction information between the issuing bank and the merchant's acquiring bank. The network and issuing institutions are responsible for enforcing transaction policies and rules and handling final settlement. Most notably, they employ network tokenization technology, replacing the original card number with a merchant-specific token. These tokens act as secure substitutes for the underlying card and prevent the card's token from being used by other merchants (which is highly beneficial for agent control). These networks are deeply optimized for consumer protection. Refund, fraud revocation, and dispute resolution mechanisms have been refined over decades and operate on a large scale globally. If an agent books a flight and the route changes, the consumer has recourse. If a stolen bank card is used for shopping, the issuing bank can reverse the fraudulent transaction. If a consumer buys a laptop online but never receives it, they can dispute the charge and recover the funds. This layer of security is the cornerstone of how bank card payment systems operate. Another advantage is merchant acceptance. Visa and Mastercard are accepted at over 150 million locations worldwide. Stablecoin acceptance is only a tiny fraction of that, primarily limited to cryptocurrency-native merchants and specific payment processors. For any agent performing tasks within the existing economic system—such as booking travel, ordering supplies, paying SaaS bills, or purchasing software licenses—bank cards are the obvious choice. Finally, bank card networks possess a psychological and economic lever that stablecoins currently lack: consumer rewards. In the US and other mature markets, the status of "preferred payment method" is earned through cash back, airline miles, and loyalty points. These rewards are funded by transaction fees ($0.30 + 2-3%) that make payment gateways expensive. Bank cards essentially "incentivize" users to use them rather than other alternatives, especially in high-value transactions. Recent protocols are expanding this existing structure to more directly support AI agents. Stripe and OpenAI's Agent Commerce Protocol (ACP), Visa's Trusted Agent Protocol (TAP), and Mastercard's Agent Pay are all extensions to the existing bank card ecosystem designed to make automated transactions (by agents) trustworthy. ACP focuses on enabling agents to transact through existing merchant payment processes by integrating identity, authorization, and payment execution into a standardized protocol that merchants and payment providers can verify. Visa's TAP framework is designed to work in conjunction with its Visa Token Service (VTS), allowing merchants to verify whether a purchase was executed on behalf of the user by an AI agent authorized by the user. Mastercard's Agent Pay also leverages tokenization and programmable spending controls, allowing issuers to bind payment tokens to transaction limits, authorization windows, and predefined usage policies to ensure agents do not exceed their permissions or intentions. In practice, these approaches converge on a similar pattern: AI agents use tokenized bank card credentials for transactions, while the protocol layer verifies the agent's identity and permissions. Spending limits, authorization windows, and other policy controls are then enforced by the issuing bank, which can directly bind these rules to the payment credentials. The result is a system in which agents can operate autonomously while still being bound by the same rules, controlled by the card issuer, that are still used today to manage bank cards. Therefore, "just use a bank card" is the right advice for most agentic tasks. If your agent is doing what humans would do, then use the infrastructure that humans are using. But agents are not just faster humans. They are another type of economic actor, and this changes what transactions might occur. Humans wouldn't fill out a payment form for a $0.003 API call because the friction cost outweighs the value itself. Nor would humans approve 1,000 microtransactions per hour, or continuously rebalance positions every 30 seconds. These transactions don't exist in human commerce not because they're technically infeasible, but because no one would bother. Intelligent agents eliminate this friction. Cognitive overhead is reduced to zero. However, eliminating human friction is not the same as eliminating economic friction. Bank card channels have a fixed cost floor: approximately $0.30 to $0.50 per transaction, plus a 2-3% fee. This bottom line exists because each layer of a multi-party settlement architecture takes a cut: the issuing bank, the acquiring bank, the card network, and the processor. This cannot be eliminated through engineering design. Furthermore, bank card fees vary depending on card type, merchant category, and transaction volume. For example, with 1000 API calls per hour, a smart agent using a bank card would pay approximately $300 to $500 in processing fees. A stablecoin smart agent using the x402 protocol would pay less than $1 to $10 (on Solana or Base). In this new type of transaction, the buyer protection model is also reversed. The bank card network prioritizes the buyer: the seller bears the costs of fraud protection, refund risk, and compliance expenses. Stablecoins are seller-first: settlement is final, the seller gains certainty, and the buyer waives recourse. This reversal is usually a disadvantage for stablecoins. However, it becomes an advantage when certain conditions are met: Buyers don't need protection. Transaction amounts are extremely small, delivery is instant and verifiable, or payment is voluntary. The burden on sellers at the bank card level is high enough to completely impede transactions: minimum fees per transaction exceed the payment amount, refunds for consumed digital goods would result in irreparable losses, or sellers simply cannot access traditional payment infrastructure. (Annually, refunds cost merchants as much as $117 billion. The direct cost of a refund globally is approximately $25-33 billion, but the overall impact on merchants—including lost goods, shipping costs, processing fees, penalties, and the operational costs of handling disputes—pushes it into the $117 billion range. Refund mechanisms are particularly ineffective for digital goods and consumed services. If someone uses $50,000 worth of computing resources and then disputes that fee, those resources are exhausted. The seller not only bears the cost but now also the loss from the refund.) Intelligent agents greatly expand the areas where these two conditions overlap, for example: Small payments. A programmable agent pays a data provider $0.003 for each API call. The buyer doesn't need to provide refund protection for less than a penny; if the API returns junk data, the call can simply be stopped. Verifiable digital goods. An agent purchases datasets or computation results whose quality can be verified programmatically within milliseconds. When verification is instantaneous, you don't need refund protection. Physical goods require this protection because you need to wait days to discover if they are defective. Digital goods, however, largely do not. Metering services and elastic computing. A $5 computing task is manageable. The seller can bear the risk of refunds. But if usage surges from $5 to $50,000 in a single day, providing $50,000 worth of resources, and then there's the risk of losses due to fraud disputes, that's a real problem. Stablecoins allow you to finalize transactions in frequent, small increments. Usage can scale, but the risk of fraud borne by the seller doesn't have to scale accordingly. Long-tail cross-border services. A developer in Lagos provided a niche API that a startup in Berlin wanted to use. Sellers may not be able to use Stripe/Visa (available in many countries is limited), cannot afford the setup costs of a merchant account, or handle KYC compliance expenses for a side business. These are not marginal cases. They represent a growing business category that card payment gateways cannot structurally access. My point is simple: for agency-based businesses, cards win; for machine-native transactions that humans will never execute or that card payment gateways cannot handle, stablecoins win. Stablecoin Technology Stack: An Initial Expansion

Over the past two years, a complete technology stack has quietly emerged to support native payments by smart agents. It comprises four layers.

First is the settlement infrastructure. Bank card authorization is a complex process: from the merchant terminal to the acquiring bank, then to the card network, and finally to the issuing bank, the actual settlement takes T+1 to T+3 days to complete. For smart agents waiting for bank card authorization, each transaction takes several seconds, while funds are tied up in transit for several days.

In contrast, stablecoin transactions settle in seconds on the EVM chain and less than a second on Solana, providing instant finality rather than a pending state, which is highly advantageous for merchants. An agent can execute thousands of stablecoin transactions in the time it takes to process a single bank card payment settlement. As part of this, there's also the payment protocol: x402. x402 is a micropayment protocol native to HTTP that allows agents to pay for each API call with stablecoins. It requires no merchant account, bank card network, or acquiring bank. Coinbase's implementation on Solana costs $0.00025 per transaction. Since May 2025, over 50 million transactions have been settled across all supported chains. x402 is a great start, and I expect new infrastructure solutions to emerge in the near future. Secondly, there's the wallet and policy infrastructure. Bank cards excel at permission management, but stablecoins can do the same with the help of a wallet-layer policy engine. Coinbase's Agentic Wallets (launching in February 2026) implements gas-free transactions and programmable spending limits on Base. Furthermore, policy layers led by Turnkey, Privy, and Safe allow developers to directly encode dynamic allow lists, spending caps, and merchant-level routing rules into the wallet. These safeguards, similar to the bank card technology stack, ensure that agent permissions are constrained by the infrastructure, rather than relying on the reliability of the LLM/skills/orchestrator itself. Finally, the most innovative but yet fully utilized part is agent identity: ERC-8004. The Trustless Agents standard (launching in January 2026) is working to build an on-chain reputation infrastructure for smart agents, and in its first few months, 83,000 smart agents have registered on 18 networks. Its goal is to achieve native credit scoring for smart agents, as under-collateralized credit does not yet exist on-chain, but I believe ERC-8004 will bring about change.

Built for missions, not for factions

The market has already foreshadowed this shift. In February 2026, ACP architect Stripe added support for x402 to Base, effectively connecting smart agents that natively use bank cards with stablecoin settlements. ...p>

data.title: How will the wave of redemptions from private credit funds affect the financial and crypto markets?
data.description:
Written by: jsai@Jinse Finance

In March 2026, in a corner unseen by the US-Iran war, the US private lending market suddenly faced a peak in redemption pressure.

BlackRock's flagship fund, the HPS Corporate Lending Fund (HLEND), with $26 billion in assets under management, announced that it would strictly limit quarterly redemptions to 5%, despite investor redemption requests reaching 9.3% (approximately $1.2 billion in requests, with only about $620 million approved).

This is not an isolated case; funds under leading institutions such as Blackstone, Blue Owl, Cliffwater, and Morgan Stanley have also faced similar predicaments.

I. Which funds have already implemented redemption restrictions?

Currently, it has not reached the point of a "general" industry-wide mandatory shutdown of redemptions, but the pressure is highly concentrated on "semi-open" or interval funds (BDC) targeting retail and high-net-worth clients.

These funds typically offer a redemption window of up to 5% of net asset value each quarter. If applications exceed this limit, the manager can legally initiate a restriction mechanism. Funds known to have begun restricting redemptions include: BlackRock HPS Corporate Lending Fund (HLEND, $26 billion): Applications in Q1 2026 were 9.3%, strictly limited to 5%, triggering the "structural cap" for the first time. This is also the first large institution to explicitly restrict perpetual private lending products since the recent market turmoil. Blackstone BCRED (approximately $82 billion): Applications reached a record 7.9%, exceeding the usual 5% cap. The company used approximately $400 million of its own funds and those of its executives to fully meet redemptions, without officially "closing the gate," but demonstrating significant buffering pressure. Blue Owl Capital's OBDC II and other BDCs ($1.6-1.9 billion): Starting in February, regular quarterly redemptions ceased, replaced by periodic distributions (loan repayments or asset sales), effectively a restriction. Previously, it had repurchased 15.4% of its holdings, and the stress test revealed its first cracks. Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund ($33 billion): Facing redemption requests exceeding 7%, the window just closed on Tuesday (March 10th), and the company has not yet decided whether to limit it to 5% or increase it to 7%. Morgan Stanley North Haven Private Income Fund ($8 billion): With requests around 11%, North Haven returned approximately $169 million after setting a 5% redemption cap, less than half of the investor redemption requests. Other funds such as Ares and Fidelity have also seen increased redemption rates, but have not yet officially imposed restrictions. Overall, in the $1.8-2 trillion private lending market, retail-oriented funds are the hardest hit, while institutional funds are relatively stable, but the risk of confidence spillover has already emerged.

II. Why Restrict Redemptions: The Root Cause Lies in Structural Mismatch and Market Concerns

The core selling point of private lending is "high returns + low volatility + quarterly liquidity," but the underlying assets are long-term, non-public, and difficult-to-liquidate corporate loans (especially SaaS, technology LBOs, etc.).

Although many funds have explicitly stipulated a redemption window of up to 5% of net asset value, restricting redemptions is compliant and legal, when a wave of redemptions hits, funds face the shattering of their "liquidity illusion."

Factors triggering the redemption wave include: AI Impact: Many funds heavily invest in software company mortgage loans; generative AI could destroy their business models, leading to valuation downgrades. JPMorgan Chase has already downgraded the valuation of some software mortgage loans and tightened lending. Duration Mismatch: Investors can exit their funds quarterly, but loan terms are often several years. If excess redemptions are fully met, managers can only sell assets at a loss or increase leverage, harming the interests of remaining investors. BlackRock explicitly stated that "without the 5% cap, there will be a structural mismatch between investor capital and private credit loans." Loan Quality Concerns: The default rate has risen to 9.2% in 2025 (Fitch data). High-profile defaults (such as the UK's MFS fraud bankruptcy, with risk exposure exceeding £2 billion) exacerbate panic. Retail investor runs: In the past few years, retail investors' entry into the market has driven up its size. Now, with the Fed's interest rate cuts, the impact of AI, and macroeconomic uncertainty, retail investors are the first to withdraw, creating a "first come, first served" dilemma. When faced with a wave of redemptions, fund managers also face a dilemma. Fully satisfying redemptions would damage their reputation and long-term discipline; strict restrictions could trigger more panic redemptions. Blackstone chose to "pay out of its own pocket" to buffer the losses, BlackRock adhered to the terms, and Cliffwater is still weighing its options. III. Impact on financial markets: Systemic risks are emerging, similar to the eve of 2007? Private lending has become the core of "shadow banking," approaching $2 trillion in size. If a liquidity crisis spreads, it will have the following impacts: Asset management giants' stock prices plummet: BlackRock's stock price fell more than 7% after its announcement, and alternative asset management firms such as Blackstone, KKR, and Ares are collectively under pressure, marking their worst performance in a decade this year. Asset sell-offs and deleveraging: If more funds are forced to sell loans at bargain prices, it will depress valuations in the public bond market, and banks will tighten leverage financing for private lending (JPMorgan Chase has already taken action). Confidence crisis spillover: The collapse of retail investor confidence may affect the entire alternative asset class, leading to increased discounted trading in the secondary market. Analysts have likened this to the "canary moment" of the 2007 subprime crisis—an early sign of liquidity depletion and valuation opacity. In the short term, managers can avoid an immediate collapse by using their own funds or selling assets as a buffer; however, if the default rate continues to rise or the economy slows down, systemic risks will be amplified. IV. Impact on the Crypto Market: Indirect Transmission Predominates, RWA Becomes a Key Channel The direct impact of the private lending crisis on the crypto market is limited, but there are two spillover paths: Macroeconomic Risk Aversion and Deleveraging: Private lending deleveraging may trigger a wider sell-off of risky assets, with high-beta assets such as Bitcoin bearing the brunt. Historical experience shows that traditional financial liquidity tightening is often accompanied by a crypto "de-risking" market. RWA and On-Chain Transmission in DeFi: The current on-chain private lending scale is close to $5 billion, mainly entering DeFi in the form of tokenized lending. If the underlying traditional loans are impaired or defaulted, fluctuations in RWA net worth will trigger DeFi liquidations and liquidity tightening. Analysts warn that this could be transmitted to the crypto ecosystem through two channels: "macro deleveraging + tokenized credit products." However, overall, the crypto market is more affected by "emotional contagion" than by direct asset linkages. Short-term volatility may increase, but long-term performance depends on Federal Reserve policy and the actual implementation of AI economy. This also serves as a warning that the crypto market must pay close attention to the underlying assets of RWA during the process of putting real-world assets (RWA) on-chain. Once the underlying assets encounter problems, the so-called RWA tokenization and DeFi will be castles in the air. Conclusion: Beware of "liquidity illusion," private lending enters a stress test period. This wave of redemptions is not the end of private lending, but rather its first real liquidity test after rapid expansion. While the adherence to caps by giants like BlackRock has protected funds in the short term, it tests investor trust in the long run. Financial markets need to pay attention to subsequent quarterly reports (more funds will disclose their results in March and April), while the crypto market should closely monitor RWA default signals and macro liquidity. For investors, it is advisable to carefully assess their own liquidity needs and avoid chasing the "high-yield illusion." Regulators may strengthen retail access scrutiny to prevent excessive risk transfer to retail investors. Whether this crisis is a "canary in the coal mine" or a "temporary pain" will be answered by data in 3-6 months. Markets always seek balance amidst volatility—the next chapter in private lending may well be a more transparent and sustainable one.

data.title: Those born after 1995 are already "old": AI recruitment is "burying" the middle management.
data.description:
Author: Ada, Deep Tide, TechFlow

“A major internet company offered 60 AI-background PhD graduates with annual salaries exceeding 3 million RMB this year.” Xiao Mafeng, founder of TTC, a headhunting firm that has served over 1500 AI companies, stated this number in a very calm tone, as if reporting the day's temperature.

In the same month, data from Maimai showed that the number of AI job postings surged 29 times, and Zhaopin.com reported a 200% increase in job seekers. 29 times more job postings, 200% influx—the numbers look like a bull market candlestick chart.

But these figures conceal a secret: a massive influx of capital and attention is flowing into a funnel with an extremely narrow opening. A few dozen individuals at the top have inflated salary expectations across the entire market, while hundreds of thousands at the bottom bear the brunt of all the anxiety. Meanwhile, in the middle of the funnel, those who have been in the workforce for five or ten years are being quietly drained. The booming talent market is a facade; the illusion of liquidity is real. A skilled general is hard to find, while countless soldiers fight tooth and nail. A Liepin report shows that 47% of AI positions require a master's or doctoral degree, and nearly half of the companies only recognize graduates from 985/211 universities. Headhunter Eva put it more bluntly: "Big companies are hiring; a 211 university degree is barely acceptable, at least a 985 university degree is required, and they basically don't even look at resumes without vertical project experience." What does the top tier look like? On the day the news of Lin Junyang's departure from Alibaba's Qianwen was released, "people from all sorts of big companies came to us, asking if we could help them contact Lin Junyang," Xiao Mafeng recalled. There are probably only a few dozen talents of this caliber nationwide. To find them, headhunters have long since stopped looking at resumes. They spend their time on GitHub checking code commit records, following paper authors on Google Scholar, and infiltrating podcast listener groups and AI startup communities. Eva even joined an AI startup competition group at Tsinghua University, which was full of young people in their late twenties. "We're talking now so that when they need jobs in two or three years, we can reserve a spot." Another headhunter, Steve, who started recruiting in the AI ​​field in 2022, made a thought-provoking remark: "I seriously doubt that there will be no resumes in the future." He gave an example. In January of this year, a company wanted to hire someone knowledgeable in OpenClaw. The field was too new, and no one would put it on a resume. His approach was to break down the requirement. Essentially, it was a multi-agent framework problem. Had anyone developed a similar framework? Was this framework open source? Who were the contributors in the open-source community? Resumes are depreciating in value, and traditional recruitment channels are becoming ineffective. Someone seized an opportunity from this crack. DINQ co-founder Sam's entrepreneurial journey began with a similar observation: the top authors of OpenAI papers were often not from prestigious universities, some even dropped out, were young, lacked academic titles, and were not easily understood by those outside the tech world. LinkedIn's logic of looking at education and resumes doesn't work for AI talent. Therefore, Sam created DINQ, a "LinkedIn for AI scientists and developers," focusing on achievements rather than resumes: top conference paper citations, GitHub code contributions, and whether collaborators are top-tier technical experts. When HR enters "Sora 2" on DINQ, the platform expands to include authors of papers related to that technology, rather than limiting itself to Sora 2-related experience, thus uncovering hidden talents. Xiao Mafeng's alternative is "build in public": simply release your product directly; that's the best proof of your capabilities. Although 621 universities have already established undergraduate programs in artificial intelligence, McKinsey predicts a 4 million AI talent gap in China by 2030. However, the term "gap" is misleading. What's lacking are experimental scientists who have trained on 100,000 GPUs, and multi-skilled talents who understand the limits of large-scale models and can find commercial applications. People who say "I'm particularly interested in AI" after listening to two podcast episodes are never in short supply in the market. Ye Xiangyu, founder of Nowcoder, aptly summarizes it: at the top, "a general is hard to find," while at the bottom, "ten thousand soldiers fight tooth and nail." The statement on Maimai that "one suitable candidate can be matched for every two AI positions" refers to the top. What about the bottom? No one keeps track of it because resumes at the bottom of the pyramid don't even get a chance to enter the system. Leveraged Pricing: The Closer to the Model, the More Valuable. So where does the money actually go? Eva provided some figures. At a major company, a P7 level non-technical position has a ceiling of around 1 million RMB. A comparable AI technical position earns 1.5 million to 2 million RMB. The difference in salary increases between job hopping is even greater; a 50% increase is common for technical positions, and some even double; non-technical positions increase by 10% to 20%, with a maximum of no more than 30%. Steve used one word to explain this pricing logic: leverage. Imagine the model as the sun. The closer someone is to the core, the greater the leverage they can exert, and the higher their value. An improvement in model capabilities made by a core researcher could impact a large company's market capitalization by billions. The cost of the 100,000 cards they operate far exceeds their own salary. From this perspective, even giving them 100 million wouldn't be expensive. What about those farther from the sun? Product managers, operations, and sales—the leverage effect isn't as direct, and their salaries are naturally limited. Steve estimates that at the application layer, the salary gap between technical and non-technical positions is more than two to three times. Xiao Mafeng adds a crucial variable. He believes this "hierarchy of contempt" is essentially supply and demand, and it has two layers. On a macro level, only a handful of people have done tens of thousands of calories of training, and their salaries are astronomical. But on a micro level, it depends on the founding team's DNA. If the founder is a professor at Tsinghua University, there are plenty of technical talents in the lab, but those who can commercialize the technology are more valuable. The scarcity of a few dozen people defines the salary narrative of the entire industry. Those who follow this narrative as a benchmark only find a gap between their expectations and reality. A purge of "Old Deng" "The AI ​​era rejects Old Deng," Xiao Mafeng offered a sharp commentary. Those who were in the last wave of AI, experiencing the era of Megvii and SenseTime, are now probably in their forties, and their experience has become a burden. Steve's statement was more subtle but consistent: "We don't believe in finding new continents with old maps. People who have worked in an industry for too long have great potential and inertia. The brain's direct reaction is the result of intensive training, but times have changed, and the correct reaction may be completely the opposite." Age anxiety has permeated every level. Some investment institutions are hunting for Gen Z entrepreneurs, and statements like "Those born after 1995 are already old" are starting to appear. This sounds absurd, but the signal from the recruitment market is very real: when resources are limited, the scales tip unhesitatingly towards young people. "Now the competition is about the speed of execution and implementation; everyone is training special forces, not large formations," Steve said. Special forces don't need so many commanders. But here's a contradiction no one wants to answer directly. What truly brings AI products to market and transforms technology into commercial value is precisely industry experience, tacit knowledge, and the lessons learned from past mistakes. Steve himself admits that this tacit knowledge exists in more mature individuals. They may not know which path they'll take, but they know which paths are definitely not viable. The industry needs the drive of young people, but it also needs the judgment of veterans. Everyone can say these two things, but the flow of money only addresses the first half. The middle layer has been swallowed up. Three headhunters unanimously mentioned a change: Management is being compressed. "It's already quite difficult for pure managers. A lot of things are being disrupted; the system you built might be overturned tomorrow," said Steve. Organizations are becoming extremely flat, no longer needing the pyramid structure of layered reporting, but rather small teams where everyone can fight. Relying on an agent to do something is less effective than relying on a person to do it. The emphasis used to be on strong management skills and managing complex teams, but this is being challenged. The boundaries between product managers, operations, and front-end/back-end engineers are blurring. One person can run an entire product's MVP using AI. Chen Lei (pseudonym) worked as a product director at a mid-sized AI company for three years, managing an eight-person team. At the beginning of this year, the company restructured, and her team was disbanded; four people were reassigned to Agent products, and two were laid off. Her own title changed from "director" to "senior product manager," reporting to a technical lead five years her junior. “I wasn’t laid off, but I know this is even worse than being laid off,” she said. “What you’ve built up over three years at this company is gone with just one organizational restructuring. And you can’t complain, because people will say, ‘Aren’t you still here?’” This is the cruelest part of this illusion of job mobility. At the top of the funnel, dozens of geniuses are being snapped up at exorbitant prices. At the bottom, hundreds of thousands of newcomers can’t even get in the door. And in the middle of the funnel, those who have been in the workplace for five, ten, or even fifteen years are being sidelined from within. Several rungs of the career ladder have been removed. Before, you could take an elevator, going up floor by floor; now it’s like skydiving—either landing directly at the top or freefalling. Who is creating this illusion? Who benefits from this illusion of liquidity? Recruitment platforms use headlines like "AI job postings surge 29 times" and "talent gap of 4 million" to garner traffic, pushing more anxious job seekers into the funnel with every repost. Companies use AI as a fig leaf. Forrester Research found that 55% of employers admit to regretting layoffs due to AI, as the AI ​​skills being replaced were never prepared. A survey by Resume.org is even more direct: 59% of companies admit to packaging layoffs as "AI-driven," arguing it sounds better to stakeholders. Saying it's because of AI sounds like a strategic upgrade; saying it's because of poor performance sounds like management failure. AI has become the best fig leaf. Klarna laid off 700 people, saying AI replaced customer service, but service quality plummeted, customers revolted, and they were quietly brought back. This is not an isolated case. Forrester predicts that half of the AI ​​layoffs will eventually be quietly rehired, but at lower salaries, or outsourced overseas. Steve aptly summarized the current mindset of bosses: "The first question they ask now is, 'Should we hire?' And then, what kind of people should we hire?" According to Forrester Research, only 16% of employees globally have high AI readiness. Companies don't invest in training; employees rely on self-learning. Generation Z has the highest AI readiness at 22%, yet they are the first to be eliminated from entry-level positions, because these are precisely the positions most easily swallowed up by AI. A Mercer survey shows that employee anxiety about AI-induced unemployment will surge from 28% in 2024 to 40% in 2026. AI is both a reason to poach talent and an excuse to lay off employees. Whoever holds the power to define it is the dealer in this game. The funnel won't widen. Let's go back to the initial set of numbers. A 29-fold increase in jobs, a 200% surge in job seekers, an annual salary of 3 million, and a talent gap of 4 million. Each number is real, but put together, they tell a completely different story: jobs are increasing, but the pool is extremely narrow; job seekers are flooding in, but the vast majority can't even pass the initial screening; salaries are soaring, but only a few dozen people at the very top of the pyramid receive them; the talent gap is widening, and the supply and demand are completely mismatched. But this funnel won't widen. AI technology iterates every six months; what's hottest today might be obsolete in six months. You think you're close to the sun, but a new model release can leave you relegated to the periphery. Steve said something that could serve as both an epitaph and an entry ticket for the industry: "Measuring seniority by length of time is probably no longer enough. What matters is the density and depth of your interaction with AI. Some people entered the industry four years ago but only used it casually. Some people only joined last year but are fully committed. Tell me, who has more seniority?" The three headhunters themselves are also being reshaped by this industry. Eva is learning algorithm principles, Steve is researching Agent frameworks, and Xiao Mafeng just came from a conference for Gen Z entrepreneurs, marveling that "their understanding has reached the next level." Even those selling shovels have to keep up with the pace of gold mining. Chen Lei recently started a small project on GitHub, writing an automatic legal document generation tool using the Agent framework. No one asked her to do it, and no one paid her. She said she'd figured something out: rather than waiting to be filtered through the funnel, it's better to carve out your own hole. This might be the only slightly optimistic part of the whole text, but even that's only marginally optimistic. The vast majority of people are neither among the 60 PhDs who received 3 million yuan, nor are they like Chen Lei, who have the ability and willingness to carve out their own holes. They are the silent majority in the middle of the funnel; those who aren't top-tier enough can be snapped up at exorbitant prices, and those who aren't decisive enough can start over. This funnel won't widen.

data.title: Jay Clayton's Crypto Wars
data.description:
Author: Lucas Matney Source: bankless Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Elizabeth Warren's style is refreshingly straightforward. The senator openly admits her aversion to the crypto industry, and her actions confirm this. She makes no secret of being an enemy of the industry.

But Jay Clayton is completely different.

This Trump-appointed Attorney for the Southern District of New York continues to launch a series of cases, clearly demonstrating his hostility towards the crypto industry and its developers. Yet, with the president's full support ("Jay Clayton is brilliant!!!"), almost no one in the industry is willing to publicly criticize him by name.

Jay's Legacy of Crypto Enforcement

Since Trump's first term as Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, Jay Clayton has never truly stood on the side of the crypto industry:

  • Long before Gary Gensler systematized it, he pioneered a "enforcement-over-regulation" model for crypto regulation.

  • He relentlessly cracked down on ICO fundraising, forcing the industry into an era of airdrops and points, from which we are only recently slowly emerging.

  • He seized every opportunity to block the approval of a Bitcoin ETF.

  • Before taking over Gensler, Clayton's last action was to sue Ripple, accusing it of issuing securities without registration (while the court ultimately ruled that XRP itself was not a security). In short, Jay Clayton paved the way for Gensler. However, the difference is that Gensler's era of influence has ended, and Trump reappointed Clayton to a senior position last year. Despite Trump's public calls for regulators to take a completely opposite approach during his second term, Clayton continues to pursue enforcement actions against crypto developers. This reality was highlighted again yesterday: Jay Clayton requested a retrial of two charges against Roman Storm, the developer of Tornado Cash. Storm is the developer of the crypto mixing protocol. Although the 2023 indictment against the Tornado Cash developers was initiated by officials during the Biden administration, Jay Clayton, after taking office, spared no effort in sending the project's developers to prison. This all seems somewhat absurd, because in 2025, the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) lifted sanctions on the Tornado Cash smart contract, which was the prerequisite for the Southern District of New York prosecutor to indict the developers. Logically, Clayton should have dropped the charges by then, but he didn't. Trump has never publicly commented on the case, and crypto and AI giant David Sachs has remained silent. In August, Storm was convicted of operating an unlicensed money transfer business (Clayton also forced the Samorai Wallet developers to plead guilty to the same charge last year), but the jury failed to reach a unanimous verdict on money laundering and dismissed the sanctions circumvention charges. Now, Jay Clayton wants to do it all over again—attempting to set a disastrous legal precedent for crypto developers. We must speak out more loudly. It's commendable that almost all major crypto industry lobbying groups are currently investing heavily in protecting crypto developers and fighting to end the abuse of federal funds transfer permit regulations. But we need to hear more from crypto executives, investors, and builders. Despite Trump's large following and numerous donors in the crypto world supporting his campaign and his family's various crypto businesses, industry leaders have seemed cautious in using their political capital over the past year. Understandably, most have only publicly voiced support for issues affecting their own businesses, remaining almost completely silent on the Roman Storm case. Take a look at this (unofficial) list compiled by *The Defiant*: attendees of the Trump White House Crypto Summit in March 2025. Many of these 25 "industry heavyweights" have been very close to the Trump administration over the past year. However, after checking each one, I found that only one executive on the list publicly tweeted in support of Roman Storm. Even if I may have missed some public statements or podcast remarks, to the best of my ability, the only executive on the list who personally tweeted in support of Roman Storm's case is Matt Huang of Paradigm. His venture capital firm publicly voiced its support and directly donated to Storm's defense fund (Bankless also provided a donation). Trump proclaims himself the crypto president, which is beneficial to the industry as a whole. We didn't get everything we wanted, but at least in the initial legislative process, we received relatively fair treatment. However, even if Trump's appointees embraced crypto as a technology and asset class, it doesn't mean they understood the industry's core values. We have a responsibility to clearly communicate: we are a values-driven industry, and we expect a pro-crypto government that doesn't attack or erode those values. Privacy is not a crime. Developing open-source software is not a crime. Jay Clayton must hear this. More leaders in our industry must say it.

data.title: Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs postpone expectations of Fed rate cuts: later but more significant cuts.
data.description:
Author: Xiao Yanyan, Jinshi Data

Morgan Stanley stated that the Federal Reserve may resume rate cuts as early as June, but there is a risk that the oil price shock triggered by the Iran war will delay the next rate cut.

The bank's economists maintain their previous forecast that the Fed will implement two 25-basis-point rate cuts this year—in June and September, respectively, although rising energy prices may exacerbate inflation. However, they believe that the Fed may postpone the first rate cut to September or even December, both of which could push the next rate cut to 2027.

Morgan Stanley's chief U.S. economist, Michael Gapen, and his colleagues said in a report on Wednesday, "If the Fed listens to historical experience, ignores the price pressures from oil, and implements easing policies sooner than expected, then we believe we are in a good position." The U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran significantly boosted oil prices and impacted markets. This has led to doubts about when the Fed will begin easing monetary policy again; the futures market currently expects only one 25-basis-point rate cut this year, most likely at the October meeting. Despite U.S. President Trump's recent repeated statements that the war with Iran will end "soon," and the International Energy Agency's (IEA) agreement on Wednesday to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from emergency reserves, oil prices remain high, with Brent crude trading at around $90 per barrel. Morgan Stanley economists say that if oil prices fail to fall back to pre-conflict levels, the impact on overall inflation in 2026 will be greater, and the unemployment rate will remain slightly high until the end of 2028. Before Trump launched the war, oil prices were around $70 per barrel. Gapen and his colleagues stated, "The Fed will balance this contradiction in its dual mandate by easing policy to a greater extent than originally planned. Therefore, our second risk to the monetary policy outlook is that the Fed will cut rates later, but more." Economists stated, "In our view, current market pricing reflects a high degree of uncertainty related to the duration of the conflict, as well as a recognition that the Fed's response will only become clearer with the passage of data and time." Goldman Sachs also postponed its forecast for Fed rate cuts, now expecting 25 basis point cuts in September and December, citing rising inflation risks related to the Middle East conflict. Previously, the bank had expected rate cuts to begin in June, followed by another cut in September. Goldman Sachs stated in a report on Wednesday that "based on our higher revised inflation forecasts, starting rate cuts in June appears premature," adding that earlier rate cuts are still possible if the labor market weakens earlier and more severely than expected.

data.title: Ripple Rolls Out $750 Million Tender Offer to Repurchase Shares From Early Backers as Company Value Rises to $50 Billion
data.description:
Ripple Defies Market Gravity With 750 Million Dollar Share Buyback

While the broader digital asset market feels the chill of a prolonged downturn, Ripple is turning up the heat.

The San Francisco-based firm has launched a tender offer to repurchase up to $750 million of its shares from early investors and employees.

This move does more than just provide a payday for long-term backers; it effectively values the blockchain payments leader at $50 billion.

The offer, which is slated to wrap up in April 2026, signals a firm that is comfortable betting on itself even as competitors tighten their belts.

📈BUYBACK: @RIPPLE LAUNCHES $750M SHARE BUYBACK

Per @business, Ripple has launched a $750 million share buyback program, offering to repurchase equity from early investors at a valuation of about $50 billion.

The move gives long-time shareholders and employees a chance to sell… pic.twitter.com/TvWJmdu20g

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 11, 2026

Why Is Ripple Increasing Its Valuation During A Crypto Slump

The timing of this buyback has caught the eye of industry analysts because it contradicts the current pricing of the underlying crypto market.

Since October 2025, Bitcoin has retreated more than 40% from its peak, and Ripple’s own associated token, XRP, has seen a value drop between 30% and 40%.

Despite this, the $50 billion price tag on the company’s equity represents a 25% jump from its 40 billion dollar valuation just a few months ago.

In November 2025, the company secured $500 million from a heavyweight roster of investors, including Citadel Securities, Fortress Investment Group, and Galaxy Digital.

Swell 2025: We have closed a $500 million strategic investment at a $40 billion valuation, led by Fortress Investment Group and Citadel Securities: https://t.co/orsBjdkWbE

→ $95B+ in total Ripple Payments payment volume
→ $1B+ $RLUSD stablecoin market cap
→ 6 strategic…

— Ripple (@Ripple) November 5, 2025

By offering to buy back shares at a premium now, Ripple appears to be nudging employees and stakeholders who were previously hesitant to sell.

A similar 1 billion dollar attempt in September 2025 saw low participation, but the new, higher price point aims to provide the liquidity that early supporters have been waiting for.

How Does This Impact Ripple Position In The Global Market

Ripple has evolved far beyond a simple cross-border payment utility.

Over the past year, the company has transformed into a diversified financial infrastructure powerhouse through a massive acquisition spree.

It committed $1.25 billion to bring the prime brokerage Hidden Road into the fold and spent another 1 billion dollars on the treasury management firm GTreasury.

These moves, alongside the $200 million purchase of the stablecoin platform Rail, have allowed Ripple to build a comprehensive ecosystem for institutional finance.

In 2025…

Ripple Acquisitions:
• Hidden Road: $1.25 billion ✅
• Rail: $200 million ✅
• GTreasury: $1 billion ✅

Ripple Buyback:
• $1 billion in XRP ✅

Support:
• Evernorth, alongside SBI Japan, purchasing over $1 billion in XRP for treasury ✅

Additional… pic.twitter.com/Co1Bt9po9o

— Josiah Gallegos (@josiahmarqus) October 22, 2025

The firm’s own stablecoin, RLUSD, has already hit a market cap of $1.57 billion, proving there is significant appetite for Ripple-branded dollar assets.

Meanwhile, its core payment network has now processed over $100 billion in transactions, cementing its role as a critical bridge for banks and service providers moving money across borders.

Is An Initial Public Offering On The Horizon For Ripple

Despite the massive valuation and the steady stream of acquisitions, a traditional stock market debut does not seem to be the immediate goal.

Ripple president Monica Long confirmed earlier this year that "an IPO is not currently planned," suggesting the company is content to remain private while it expands its footprint in the Asia-Pacific region.

RIPPLE $XRP PRESIDENT MONICA LONG: "WE DO NOT HAVE AN IPO TIMELINE," pic.twitter.com/nJbolr9BL0

— The Wolf Of All Streets (@scottmelker) November 6, 2025

This expansion includes a pending deal for BC Payments to secure an Australian Financial Services License.

While the company stays private for now, leadership remains incredibly optimistic about the scale of the industry.

In February 2026, CEO Brad Garlinghouse shared his conviction about the sector's growth, saying he believes a "trillion-dollar crypto company is an inevitability."

With XRP sitting as the fifth-largest cryptocurrency and spot XRP ETFs drawing $1.26 billion in inflows since November 2025, Ripple is positioning itself to be the primary contender for that title.

data.title: Understanding the "five-layer cake" of AI reveals the secrets to wealth over the next decade.
data.description:
Author: Chen Xiaomeng; Source: X, @MengLayer

The market's understanding of AI is still too superficial. Many people simply see it as a useful little tool or a smart chat software, which is a huge mistake.

In Lao Huang's vision, AI has long transcended the realm of simple software. It has completely become a fundamental social infrastructure like electricity and the internet. He has extremely precisely divided this ongoing computing power revolution into "five layers of a cake."

By understanding these five layers, you can basically keep a close eye on the flow of global capital, computing power, and talent over the next decade.

Below, I will provide a detailed analysis (approximately 10,000 words) combining the investment logic of the capital market. To understand future investment directions, we must first clarify what fundamental changes have occurred in computational logic. For the past few decades, the software world was entirely pre-defined. Programmers wrote code, stored data in tables, users input commands, and computers dutifully "retrieved" and output results. SQL databases, with this access logic, dominated the previous era. Now, the rules of the game have completely changed. For the first time in human history, computers possess the ability to directly understand images, sounds, text, and even extremely ambiguous contexts. When you ask it a question, it completely skips the outdated step of searching for ready-made answers in a database. It directly "reasons" in real time and generates a completely new result on the spot. Since intelligence has become a new type of commodity that can be produced by machines in real time, the entire underlying physical architecture supporting it must be completely overhauled. The grand feast of capital lies hidden in this process of overhaul and reconstruction. Let's slice open Lao Huang's five-layer cake layer by layer and analyze the core wealth secrets of each layer. The first layer: Energy – the end of computing power is electricity, and the power grid determines the upper limit of intelligence. This is the most fundamental physical foundation of everything. Here, no software code can be cleverly manipulated; everyone must obediently bow to the laws of physics. Every time you press Enter in the dialog box, every token spit out by the large model is essentially a surge of electric current and a sharp rise in chip temperature. The process of machines generating intelligence is an extremely violent energy conversion process. Energy is the most fundamental first principle for AI development. Whether there is a sufficient scale of cheap electricity, whether the local power grid can handle it, and whether the huge heat dissipation problem can be solved—these purely physical conditions severely limit how much "intelligence" humanity can produce. Currently, AI data centers cannot be built casually; they must be extremely close to large, cheap, and extremely stable energy stations. This is why top tech giants are scrambling to acquire nuclear power plant capacity worldwide. The computing power race, in its later stages, is essentially a national-level electricity race. Investment Target Reference: Vistra (VST) / Constellation Energy (CEG): The absolute leaders in North American nuclear power and independent power producers. Large companies are building extremely power-intensive AI data centers, which traditional power grids simply cannot handle. Nuclear power, with its stable and clean baseload electricity, has become an irreplaceable commodity. Tech giants are lining up to buy electricity with multi-billion dollar long-term contracts to secure their computing power advantage. GE Vernova (GEV): A global super-giant in power generation equipment and grid infrastructure. Whether you use natural gas, wind power, or nuclear power, to deliver massive amounts of electricity to AI data centers with extreme stability, an epic upgrade of the existing aging power grid is necessary. GEV is the ultimate "shovel seller" providing transformers, gas turbines, and modernized power grid equipment. The second layer: Chips—Super Energy Converters and the Crown Jewel of Human Industry. With sufficient electricity, the next core issue is how to convert that electricity into computing power most efficiently. AI's computational demands are extremely brutal; it requires incredibly high parallel processing capabilities. At this level, simple computing power is absolutely insufficient; high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and ultra-fast interconnect channels between chips are the real deciding factor. The capabilities of a single graphics card are extremely limited; the real magic lies in connecting hundreds of thousands of graphics cards into a giant cyber brain. This requires transmitting massive amounts of data every second. Once the data transmission channel is blocked, even the most powerful computing power can only spin in vain. Every iteration of chip architecture and manufacturing process directly determines the life-or-death speed of the entire industry's expansion. The technological and financial barriers here are the highest in human history. Investment Target Reference: ASML: The crown jewel of the entire silicon-based industry. It monopolizes the global high-end EUV lithography machine market. Without ASML, this behemoth costing hundreds of millions of dollars, no matter how strong the foundry's technology, it simply cannot produce the most advanced AI chips. It is the ultimate gatekeeper that holds all advanced manufacturing processes in check. NVIDIA (NVDA): The undisputed global computing power leader. It offers far more than just chip hardware; it includes a complete set of system-level standards, including the CUDA ecosystem and NVLink interconnect technology. No matter how the underlying model changes, if you want to develop large-scale models, you must obediently pay Nvidia's "computing power tax." TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company): The world's most powerful wafer foundry. No matter how fiercely Nvidia and AMD compete, or even with Google and Microsoft developing their own chips, they all ultimately have to queue up with their blueprints and have TSMC manufacture them. It monopolizes the most advanced 3nm process and CoWoS advanced packaging capacity, firmly controlling the silicon-based foundation of the entire AI era. The third layer: Infrastructure – Super factories manufacturing "intelligence," a new type of commodity. Please completely erase the traditional concept of a data center used for "data storage" from your mind. What's rising from the ground now is a true "AI factory." This involves unimaginable amounts of land permits, dedicated power supply connections, extremely complex direct chip liquid cooling systems, and tens of thousands of fiber optic cables spanning the data center. Seamlessly connecting hundreds of thousands of top-tier graphics cards and ensuring their stable, error-free operation around the clock is an epic civil engineering feat combined with an extremely complex systems engineering challenge. These super factories are born with only one pure mission: to consume electricity day and night to manufacture "intelligence," a new type of industrial product that can be infinitely replicated. This unprecedented infrastructure boom is driving a frenzy of hardware upgrades across the board. Investment Target Reference: Super Micro (SMCI): A major assembler of core AI servers. After NVIDIA manufactures its chips, it needs an extremely strong hardware engineering team to perfectly assemble the chips, motherboards, and power supplies into deliverable, high-density AI racks, which are then directly installed in the data centers of major manufacturers. Arista Networks (ANET): The irreplaceable network nervous system for AI clusters. Previously, people were accustomed to using NVIDIA's own InfiniBand network, but now Ethernet technology is becoming increasingly popular in AI clusters. Arista is the king of high-speed Ethernet switches; both Meta and Microsoft relied heavily on its network equipment to ensure data flow when building supercomputers.

The fourth layer: The model—the digital brain that is devouring and understanding the entire physical world

After the infrastructure is built, it's time for the large model we are most familiar with to take the stage.

Don't mistakenly believe that Large Language Models (LLMs) are the end goal; language is merely a small entry point for machines to understand the world. The real disruption is spreading rapidly throughout the entire physical world. The most advanced models are now frantically absorbing and learning from biology, chemistry, meteorology, physics, and embodied robotics. Protein folding and the discovery of new materials are being compressed to the second level. Furthermore, the disruptive power of open source cannot be ignored. In the past year, open-source models have completely crossed the performance threshold. Huang specifically mentioned DeepSeek-R1 in his article. The complete open-sourcing of this inference model has directly delivered cutting-edge complex logical reasoning capabilities to developers worldwide for free. The moat of large models has been ruthlessly breached, forcing the entire industry to shift from focusing on model parameters to real-world applications. Investment Target Reference: Alphabet (GOOGL): As an absolute pioneer in the AI ​​field, Google possesses terrifying full-stack capabilities. It holds the most powerful multimodal models in the Gemini series and is aggressively expanding its developer ecosystem through the Gemma open-source model. More critically, Google has seamlessly integrated these top-tier AI capabilities into search, Android, and YouTube—super-cash cows with billions of users—building an unparalleled data and distribution moat. Microsoft (MSFT): The king of commercial applications of closed-source models. It has deeply integrated the strongest capabilities of OpenAI and cleverly embedded them seamlessly into Office 365 and Azure cloud services. In the enterprise market, where data security is paramount, Microsoft's moat is unfathomably deep. The fifth layer: Applications—the ultimate monetization pool of trillions of dollars in capital expenditure. This top layer is the true commercial arena where monetization can take place. The trillions of dollars burned in the lower four layers are all expected to be recouped here. Autonomous driving, AI drug development, humanoid robots taking over production lines, fully automated super programmer assistants… the same underlying architecture has evolved into countless disruptive business models at this layer. This layer is also the true ultimate engine of the entire AI ecosystem. Here, Jensen Huang proposes a crucial principle of business dynamics: "pull-down force." Once a killer application emerges at Layer 5 (such as an AI that can perfectly replace human lawyers in reviewing contracts), it will instantly create a massive demand black hole. This black hole will frantically devour the model reasoning capabilities of Layer 4, forcing Layer 3 to frantically expand its AI factories, ultimately passing the extreme pressure of computing power down layer by layer to the underlying chip foundries and nuclear power plants. Investment Target Reference: Palantir (PLTR): A top-tier hardcore player in B2B AI applications. While others are still developing toy-like chatbots, it has already directly integrated its AI operating system into the US military's combat command system and the top-secret production lines of Fortune 500 companies. Its AI platform helps companies directly transform the intelligence of large models into extremely real production efficiency and huge profits. Tesla (TSLA): If most tech companies dominate AI in the digital world, then Tesla is firmly targeting AI in the real physical world. Relying on the high-quality video data generated daily by millions of cars on the road, Tesla possesses an incredibly formidable real-world data barrier and edge computing power advantage in the fields of end-to-end autonomous driving (FSD) and Optimus humanoid robots. It is essentially a hardcore AI giant disguised as a car company. Summary: This five-layered pie far surpasses ordinary technological cycles. It is a complete industrial revolution reshaping energy distribution, redefining factories, and disrupting human organizational structures. Each layer is tightly interlocked, pulling each other in an extremely frantic manner. To support this colossal, rapidly spinning flywheel, we are witnessing the largest and most costly infrastructure construction frenzy in human history. Hundreds of billions of dollars have already been poured into it globally, yet the shortfall remains trillions. And this is truly just the beginning. Most of the infrastructure is still on paper, and most of the era's benefits have yet to be realized. Don't resist this epic trend; follow these five layers of logic to find your own niche.

data.title: a16z: Top 100 Global Generative AI Consumer Applications in March 2026
data.description:
Author: Olivia Moore, Partner at a16z; Translation: Shaw, Jinse Finance

Three years ago, we released the first version of this list with a simple goal: to find out which generative artificial intelligence (AI) products are truly being used by mainstream consumers. At the time, the lines between "AI-first" companies and other businesses were very clear. ChatGPT, Midjourney, and Character.AI were all products built around a foundational model, while other software companies were still figuring out how to apply the technology.

This boundary no longer exists.. CapCut, a video editing app with 736 million monthly active mobile users, relies on AI technology for its most popular features—background removal, AI effects, automatic captions, and text-to-video conversion. Canva, a design platform, builds its growth on its AI toolkit, "Magic Suite." Notion's AI-driven paid subscription rate surged from 20% to over 50% in a year, and AI features now account for about half of the company's annual recurring revenue (ARR). Starting with this edition of the list, we will expand our scope to include all consumer products where generative AI has become a core experience, including CapCut, Canva, Notion, Picsart, Freepik, and Grammarly. We believe this will more accurately reflect how people actually use AI, although the top products on the list are still predominantly native AI products.

As always, our website rankings are based on monthly unique visitors from SimilarWeb as of January 2026. Mobile apps are ranked based on monthly active users from Sensor Tower as of January 2026. Here are our core findings:

1. ChatGPT remains in the lead, but the battle for "default AI" has begun

ChatGPT remains the largest consumer AI product by a wide margin. On the web, its monthly visits are 2.7 times that of Gemini, which ranks second; on mobile, its monthly active users are 2.5 times that of Gemini. Over the past year, ChatGPT's weekly active users have increased by 500 million, reaching 900 million today. This achievement is particularly impressive considering the difficulty of maintaining growth at such a massive scale—currently, over 10% of the global population uses ChatGPT weekly.

But we are beginning to see that this area is expanding, and other general-purpose platforms are gradually emerging in specific application scenarios. Over the past year, both Gemini and Claude have seen accelerated growth in paid subscribers in the US (though still far behind ChatGPT – by this metric, ChatGPT is 8 times the size of Claude and 4 times the size of Gemini). According to Yipit Data data as of January 2026, Claude's paid subscriber growth exceeded 200% year-over-year, while Gemini grew by 258%. Furthermore, the phenomenon of using multiple platforms simultaneously is increasing – approximately 20% of weekly ChatGPT web users also use Gemini within the same week. What exactly happened? Competitors have stepped up their efforts. Google has performed strongly in the creative model field—Nano Banana generated 200 million images in its first week, bringing 10 million new users to Gemini; while Veo 3 is widely regarded as a breakthrough moment in the field of AI video. Anthropic continues to increase its efforts for high-end individual and light business users, launching Cowork, Chrome version of Claude, and Excel and PowerPoint plugins, among which Claude Code is the most noteworthy. The significance of this competition lies not only in who can currently lead, but also in who can establish an unshakeable structural advantage. Contextual data has a cumulative effect: the more a Large Language Model (LLM) knows about you, the better the results it provides, and the more frequently you will use it. Early data shows that Gemini's monthly usage on the web is climbing, but it is still 30% lower than ChatGPT (ChatGPT is 1.3 times higher); while on mobile, ChatGPT is absolutely dominant, with monthly usage 2.2 times that of Gemini. According to Yipit Data, both companies have top-tier consumer-paying user retention rates in the US market.

Next layer user lock from app store. Both ChatGPT and Claude have launched connected ecosystems—ChatGPT's custom GPTs and applications, and Claude's MCP integrations and connectors—allowing users to build workflows on top of their AI assistants. Once users configure their AI to interface with calendars, email, and customer relationship management (CRM) systems, the switching costs rise sharply. Developers may focus their efforts on the platforms with the largest user base, creating a flywheel effect similar to the platform wars of the past. We can already see the development direction of each platform. Sam Altman previously stated that OpenAI hopes to "bring AI to billions of users who cannot afford subscriptions," which is why they launched advertising; he also revealed that OpenAI will launch an identity layer of "login with ChatGPT," positioning the assistant as the default interaction point between users and the internet. The goal is to make ChatGPT the starting point for everything: shopping, booking, browsing, health management, and daily life. The application catalog already reflects this. As of late February, ChatGPT's app store covered 13 major categories and 220 apps. Claude had approximately 160 curated connectors, plus about 50 community-hosted MCP servers. However, there was only 41 overlap between the two apps, accounting for about 11% of the total number of apps, and these 41 were almost all general productivity tools: Slack, Notion, Figma, Gmail, Google Calendar, HubSpot, and Stripe. Aside from core tools, the two platforms' ecosystems are almost completely divergent. ChatGPT has over 85 apps covering travel, shopping, food, health and wellness, lifestyle, and entertainment, while Claude has almost none in these categories. These are all consumer transaction scenarios: booking flights on Expedia, ordering groceries through Instacart, browsing accommodations on Zillow, and tracking nutrition intake on MyFitnessPal. This is the most aggressive consumer-grade super-application strategy among all AI companies. Claude's exclusive integrations, on the other hand, lean towards professional fields: financial data terminals (PitchBook, FactSet, Moody's, MSCI), developer infrastructure (Sentry, Supabase, Snowflake, Databricks), scientific and medical tools (PubMed, clinical trial databases, Benchling), and the ever-growing open-source MCP community—something ChatGPT lacks. Anthropic seems to focus on heavy AI users (developers, knowledge workers, etc.). These users are currently more willing and able to pay for expensive direct subscription services. While ChatGPT also has products targeting the same group (such as Codex and Frontier), they have also expressed a vision to become a truly mainstream user platform – which could open up more monetization avenues as their user base continues to expand. They are already testing advertising, and charging commissions on transactions would be a natural expansion direction. If AI assistants are no longer just chat windows but become an operating environment, this competition may not ultimately resemble the search engine wars, where one company holds 90% of the market share, but rather a mobile operating system war: two platforms with vastly different philosophies, each building a trillion-dollar ecosystem. 2. Global Usage is Differentiating by Product Geographically, the AI ​​market is splitting into three distinct ecosystems, and the gap between them is widening.

The user groups of Western AI tools still highly overlap. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity all rank highly in the same group of countries: the United States, India, Brazil, the United Kingdom, and Indonesia, only in different orders. These products have not achieved significant usage in China and Russia. This is a result of policy: since 2022, Western technology sanctions have restricted Russia's use of US AI tools; while China requires AI service providers to register, store data domestically, and comply with content review regulations. DeepSeek is the only product to cross this barrier. Its web traffic distribution is: China (33.5%), Russia (7.1%), and the United States (6.6%), with a similar pattern on mobile. Chinese users also heavily utilize ByteDance's Doubao and the local product Kimi. Russia, which was almost invisible in our earlier rankings, has now become our third largest core region, with DeepSeek ranking second globally in penetration there. Yandex Browser, integrated with the Alice AI assistant, boasts 71 million monthly active users, ranking among the top ten global mobile AI products. Sber's GigaChat also appears on our web rankings for the first time. This pattern is similar to that in China, but the development has been much faster: sanctions created a market gap, which was filled by local products within two years. To observe the adoption of artificial intelligence from a per capita perspective, we constructed a simplified index that combines the average number of webpage views per user and the average number of monthly active mobile users per user across all products on the list, with a score range of 0-100. The results reshaped the regional landscape: Singapore ranked first, followed by the UAE, Hong Kong, and South Korea. The United States, where the vast majority of related products originated, ranked only 20th.

3. Creative Tools Play a Role

Midjourney, DALLE, and Stable Diffusion were the products that introduced most early users to generative AI—all three were launched before ChatGPT was released. Image generation tools not only dominated the creative tools space (video and audio generation came later), but also held absolute dominance in our first three lists. Since then, this category has changed dramatically. In the first ranking in September 2023, 7 out of 9 creative tools listed on the web were image generators. Three years later, only 3 image generators remained on the list, but the number of creative tools still reached 7. The difference lies in the fact that video, music, and voice products filled the gaps, taking the places vacated by image generators. The story of image-based products is essentially about ecosystem integration. With the continuous upgrades to ChatGPT's built-in native image model (GPT Image 1.5) and Gemini (nano banana), the barrier to entry for independent image generation products has been significantly raised. In the first edition of this list, Midjourney was in the top ten, but has now fallen to 46th. The remaining products—Leonardo, Ideogram, and CivitAI—are mostly geared towards specific creative communities, offering distinctive features, rather than directly competing in the general generation field. The most significant changes in this edition's list are in the video generation field. Kling AI, Hailuo, and Pixverse have all achieved real user growth, and models developed in China continue to lead in generation quality—we have every reason to expect applications based on Seedance 2.0 to appear in the next edition's list. Veo 3 is the first US model to close this gap and has brought traffic to Google Labs (its ranking rose from 36th to 25th). What's missing? Sora. In September 2025, OpenAI launched its flagship video model version 2.0 as a standalone app, allowing users to upload their digital avatars as "digital substitutes" to generate videos featuring real people. Sora stayed at the top of the US app store charts for 20 days, surpassing one million downloads faster than ChatGPT. Afterward, downloads declined as Sora no longer experienced the viral explosion of social products (currently, no one has truly integrated "AI + social"), thus failing to enter this period's mobile charts. However, according to SensorTower, Sora still has over 3 million daily active users on mobile—even as AI video creators distribute their work across platforms, they continue to use this model. The competitive advantage in the music and voice fields is even more solid. Suno (ranked 15th) maintained its position from the previous ranking. ElevenLabs has been on the list every month since September 2023, demonstrating its professional capabilities in voice cloning, dubbing, and audio production, which have not yet been simply copied as a "standard feature" by giants.
