The pattern is this: in creative fields (images, and increasingly, videos) where model giants like Google and OpenAI are focusing their efforts, the traffic for independent products is constantly being squeezed. However, products with clearer positioning, potentially higher commercialization, and serving users outside the mainstream still have room to survive. In areas where they haven't yet heavily invested (music, voice), independent products have even more opportunities. 4. The Arrival of the AI ​​Agnet Era The transformation of artificial intelligence into intelligent agents (AI Agnets) didn't begin with this list, but rather started in the previous issue's vibe coding section. When Lovable, Cursor, and Bolt appeared on our March 2025 list, they represented a completely new form: AI products no longer just answer questions or generate media content, but directly create and build for users. This is the behavior of intelligent agents in vertical scenarios. Vibe coding has proven to have a high retention rate among tech and semi-technical users. This week's list includes Replit, Lovable, and Claude Code (based on Claude). This trend has not yet truly penetrated the mass market, and there is still greater room for growth in the future. Traffic to the top five ambient coding platforms is still growing, although the initial explosive growth has slowed; however, as developers and teams increase their usage, the revenue of many of these products continues to rise. Recently, **general-purpose agents** have begun to emerge. In January 2026, an open-source project called **OpenClaw** garnered 68,000 GitHub stars and mainstream media coverage in just a few weeks, transforming from a developer's side project. Developed by Austrian developer Peter Steinberger, OpenClaw is a locally running AI agent that can integrate with your instant messaging software and perform multi-step tasks for you. If ChatGPT introduced consumers to the idea that AI could chat, then OpenClaw may be introducing them to the idea that AI can act. The product quickly gained popularity in the developer community—if our statistics were updated to February (instead of January), OpenClaw would be among the top 30 on our webpage. However, OpenClaw is (currently) not a consumer product; it requires terminal knowledge for installation and maintenance. OpenClaw continues to gain popularity among tech users, becoming the most-starred project on GitHub in early March, surpassing React and Linux. However, the product has not yet truly reached the general public—at least judging from the relatively stable number of new visits to the OpenClaw installation page. The product was acquired by OpenAI in February 2026, which may mean that a more user-friendly version of OpenClaw is coming soon.

OpenClaw is not the only general-purpose agent on the list. Manus and Genspark also made the list. Both platforms allow users to delegate open-ended tasks (research, spreadsheet analysis, PPT generation) to AI, which then completes the entire workflow end-to-end. This is Manus's second time on the list; after its initial appearance, it was acquired by Meta in December 2025 for a valuation of approximately $2 billion. Genspark, on the other hand, is a first-time entrant: the company completed a $300 million Series B funding round earlier this year and announced annualized revenue of $100 million. On mobile devices, users typically interact with the agent via text rather than a standalone app. During setup, users can connect OpenClaw to platforms like WhatsApp, Telegram, and Signal; sending it a message, like chatting with a friend, will allow it to perform tasks in the background. Other products like Poke offer similar agent experiences directly via SMS. These products will compete with the intelligent agent functions of general-purpose large-model assistants used by users daily, such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. As they build their own ecosystems through connectors and applications, will users use one of these products as their primary intelligent agent? The next six months will provide a clear answer. 5. AI Products Beyond Browsers and Apps This list has previously ranked AI products using two metrics: **web page views and mobile monthly active users**. However, a new category of AI products is emerging that cannot be covered by existing ranking metrics. The most significant growth in the consumer AI field over the past year has occurred in products for which neither of these two metrics can be statistically analyzed. The most obvious change is that **browsers themselves are becoming AI products**. Over the past nine months, OpenAI launched Atlas (a browser with ChatGPT built into every page), Perplexity released Comet, and The Browser Company, acquired by Atlassian, launched Dia. Yipit data shows that Perplexity's Comet has had the biggest impact on the market (in terms of download page views), but so far no AI browser has achieved explosive growth. Other AI giants have chosen to add AI functionality to existing browsers rather than launching standalone AI browsers. Google integrated Gemini into Chrome and released Disco, currently in beta—which dynamically generates web applications based on the user's browser tabs. Anthropic launched the Claude plugin for Chrome, which can connect to the user's Claude or Claude Code session and perform actions on web pages.

Native desktop AI tools have seen even more rapid growth, with developer tools being particularly prominent. Claude Code, a command-line AI agent for developers, achieved $1 billion in annualized revenue in just six months. OpenAI launched a standalone Mac application, Codex, which, as of early March, had 2 million weekly active users, with a weekly growth rate of 25%. Furthermore, Cursor maintained its position in the global top 50 webpage rankings. For the average consumer, the most common standalone AI desktop applications are mostly voice-related. Note-taking tools such as Fireflies, Fathom, Otter, TL;DV, and Granola reach users through a product-driven growth (PLG) model and are gradually penetrating the enterprise market; the top five products have a combined visitor count of 20 million. Office collaboration applications such as Notion (making its debut on the list this period) are also continuously deepening AI integration through features such as note-taking assistants, research agents, and even task automation. Finally, AI is increasingly being embedded in the tools people are already using. Anthropic launched Claude for Excel and Claude for PowerPoint; OpenAI released ChatGPT for Excel; and Google has deeply integrated Gemini into its entire Workspace suite—Docs, Sheets, Gmail, and Meet now all have native AI capabilities. Google also launched Personal Intelligence in January 2026, integrating Gemini with Gmail, Google Photos, YouTube, and Search, allowing the assistant to access your hotel bookings, spending records, photo albums, and viewing habits without additional commands. The significance of this list is that our rankings are increasingly underestimating the AI ​​products people actually use most frequently. A developer who spends 8 hours a day on Claude Code, a knowledge worker who dictates all their emails via Wispr, are both heavy AI users, but their behavior is rarely reflected in web traffic data. As AI moves from standalone products to built-in features, our statistical methods need to be adjusted accordingly.

data.title: OpenAI recruits the "father of lobsters" to prepare for the largest AI IPO in history.
data.description: OpenAI mobilizes the whole body
